evidence: Runtime seam layers are repeatedly modeled as function-bag structs plus pass-through methods with nil guards (e.g., RectifyRuntime wrappers in goseg/rectify/rectify.go and Runtime*Ops wrappers in goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go).

Bootstrap path duplicates the same abstraction pattern in multiple modules (main bootstrapCoordinator/bootstrapRuntimeFns/bootstrapRuntime and startuporchestrator ServerBootstrap/startupBootstrapRuntimeFns/startupBootstrapRuntime).

Config patch registry relies on string-keyed reflection (FieldByName) for patch dispatch in goseg/config/config_update_registry.go, reducing compile-time contract safety against schema changes in structs definitions.

Disk/storage behavior is tested twice with near-identical scenarios and assertions: `TestListHardDisks`, `TestIsMountedMMC`, `TestRemoveMultipartFiles`, and fstab reconciliation paths appear in both `goseg/system/disk_test.go` and `goseg/system/storage/storage_test.go`.

`goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go` combines command dispatch wrappers, transition construction, direct Docker calls, config mutation, and broadcast side effects in one file, with many one-line action wrappers and repeated transition-step scaffolding.

`goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go` centralizes a very broad set of runtime seam structs and default builders (container, urbit, snapshot, startup, load, service, image, wireguard, minio, netdata), indicating a high-coupling assembly point.

Config section handling is replicated across multiple files: per-section accessors/updaters in `goseg/structs/configs.go`, per-section patch apply helpers and field registries in `goseg/config/config_update_registry.go`, and per-section mutator registration in `goseg/config/urbit.go`.

Urbit config decoding in `goseg/structs/configs.go` silently coerces or drops invalid field types (e.g., `toInt`, `toBool`, unchecked type assertions), while `goseg/config/config_update_schema.go` rejects invalid types with explicit errors.

`goseg/rectify/rectify_services.go` mixes wrapped and unwrapped returns (`return err` for snapshot/config/update calls beside contextual `fmt.Errorf(...: %w)`), which makes equivalent failures produce different diagnostic quality.

Internal bus backpressure is handled inconsistently: `goseg/broadcast/state.go` returns `errSchedulePackBusFull`, `goseg/leak/broadcast.go` accumulates and returns drop errors, but `goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/subsystem_events.go` blocks on `eventBus <- ...` with no overflow error path.
`goseg/system/wifi_runtime_api.go` has void/no-op paths in `ConstructWifiInfo` (early returns on missing service/device) while adjacent WiFi APIs return explicit errors.

findings.summary: Runtime seam abstractions add deep call indirection with little behavioral leverage
evidence: RectifyRuntime has many methods that only call resolveRectifyRuntime, nil-check one callback, then delegate (UpdateConfig/GetContainerState/LoadUrbitConfig/.../ShipRuntimeSettingsSnapshot).

runtime_ops defines many callback-holder structs and wrapper methods (e.g., RuntimeSnapshotOps.StartramSettingsSnapshot, RuntimeConfigOps.UpdateConfig, RuntimeConfigOps.WithWgOn) that mostly forward without policy/translation.
workflowRuntime embeds RuntimeTransitionOps and then validates many callback fields, coupling workflow behavior to a large injected callback surface.
findings.suggestion: Split seams by use-case into narrow interfaces (e.g., config snapshot, urbit config mutation, transition publishing) and inject only the interface each flow needs. Resolve defaults once at constructor boundaries instead of per-method re-resolution and pass-through wrappers.

findings.summary: Bootstrap abstractions are duplicated across modules, increasing indirection and test setup cost
evidence: main.go defines bootstrapCoordinator + bootstrapRuntimeFns + bootstrapRuntime with mirrored methods and callback-missing validation.
startup_orchestrator.go repeats the same pattern with ServerBootstrap + startupBootstrapRuntimeFns + startupBootstrapRuntime.
startup_orchestrator_test.go shows high callback-construction burden to satisfy validation for tests, indicating abstraction overhead leaking into usage.
findings.suggestion: Consolidate bootstrap wiring into one reusable runtime contract (single shared seam pattern or concrete dependency struct). Provide test builders/default fixture constructors so tests override only behavior under test instead of populating large callback matrices.

findings.summary: Config patch abstraction uses stringly reflection, weakening interface honesty
evidence: config_update_registry.go resolves and mutates patch fields through reflect FieldByName in getConfigPatchValueByName/setConfigPatchValueByName.
Registry entries depend on hard-coded patchField strings (e.g., EndpointURL, WgRegistered, LinuxUpdates, GSVersion), so symbol renames can silently desync behavior until runtime.
The reflective registry is a central abstraction over config evolution/merge behavior, but it bypasses compile-time guarantees provided by concrete config structs.
findings.suggestion: Replace reflection field-name dispatch with typed field accessors (explicit getters/setters or generated registry code) so patch mapping is compiler-checked. Keep domain registry organization, but make each field spec bind to strongly typed functions rather than string field names.

findings.summary: System disk and storage test suites duplicate the same behavioral matrix, creating split ownership and high maintenance churn.
evidence: `goseg/system/disk_test.go` and `goseg/system/storage/storage_test.go` both define and assert `ListHardDisks` success/failure JSON parsing behavior.
Both suites test mmc mount detection with nearly the same partition fixtures (`TestIsMountedMMCUsesPartitionHierarchy` vs `TestIsMountedMMC`).
Both suites test multipart cleanup and fstab reconciliation idempotence/replacement flows with overlapping expected outputs.
findings.suggestion: Extract a shared table-driven contract test helper (or a shared test package) for disk/storage invariants, then keep only package-specific edge-case tests in each file to avoid mirrored test logic.

findings.summary: Configuration section design requires synchronized edits across multiple registries and wrappers for every new field/section.
evidence: `goseg/structs/configs.go` exposes per-section getters/updaters (`UpdateConnectivityConfig`, `UpdateStartramConfig`, etc.).
`goseg/config/config_update_registry.go` repeats per-section apply helpers (`applyConnectivityPatchValue`, `applyStartramPatchValue`, etc.) and section-specific field lists.
`goseg/config/urbit.go` keeps a separate per-section mutator map (`urbitSectionMutators`) with explicit registration for each section.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a single declarative section descriptor model (section id, accessor, mutator adapter, patch field map) used by all three paths so adding/changing sections is one-place metadata change.

findings.summary: Urbit operations runtime file mixes command routing, domain policy, transition orchestration, and infrastructure side effects.
evidence: `goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go` contains many wrapper actions (`togglePower`, `toggleDevMode`, `toggleNetwork`, etc.) plus low-level operations like Docker starts/deletes and config persistence in the same module.
The same file builds transition steps (`build*Steps`) while also performing direct side effects (`startram.AliasCreate`, `docker.StartContainer`, `broadcast.PublishSchedulePack`).

`goseg/shipworkflow/startram_lifecycle_runtime.go` repeats similar orchestration patterns (step assembly + config mutation + service operations), indicating duplicated orchestration style across workflows.
findings.suggestion: Split action-command mapping, transition step definitions, and infrastructure executors into separate components; keep runtime files as thin coordinators over reusable orchestration primitives.

findings.summary: A single runtime_ops assembly file aggregates too many unrelated seam definitions and default factories, weakening module boundaries.
evidence: `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go` defines numerous runtime structs and default builders across container, startup, startram, health, snapshot, config, service, image, wireguard, netdata, and minio concerns.
The same file mixes dependency schema and concrete production wiring (`buildRuntimeSeamBundle`, `defaultRuntime*`), making it both contract and composition root.
`goseg/system/wifi_runtime_api.go` follows a smaller runtime+state service pattern; the orchestration file does not maintain that separation and instead centralizes many concern families.
findings.suggestion: Split runtime seam contracts by concern into focused files/packages and move concrete wiring into a dedicated composition root; keep each runtime ops unit aligned to one subsystem boundary.

findings.summary: Manual per-key JSON decoding and per-key patch field registration create parallel mapping surfaces that are easy to drift.
evidence: `goseg/structs/configs.go` has a large key-switch in `UrbitDocker.UnmarshalJSON` mapping many string literals to fields.
`goseg/config/config_update_registry.go` separately maps patch keys/fields with repeated literal names and typed parsers.
`goseg/config/config_update_schema.go` adds another patch-key map build path (`buildConfPatchByKey`) and type parse layer, increasing independent mapping points.
findings.suggestion: Adopt a shared metadata-driven field map (with key, target field accessor, parser/default behavior) reused for unmarshal and patch application to reduce drift and centralize schema evolution.

findings.summary: Urbit JSON decoding silently accepts invalid field types instead of returning parse errors.
evidence: `UrbitDocker.UnmarshalJSON` uses permissive helpers (`toInt`, `toBool`, `toString`) and ignored assertions like `u.MinioVersion, _ = v.(string)`, so malformed values become zero/defaults without error.

The config patch path (`parseBoolValue`, `parseIntValue`, `parseStringValue` in `config_update_schema.go`) returns explicit invalid-type errors, so equivalent bad input is treated inconsistently depending on entry point.
findings.suggestion: Make `UrbitDocker.UnmarshalJSON` fail closed for type mismatches (field-scoped errors), or route decoding through a shared validation layer used by config patch parsing so both paths enforce the same error contract.

findings.summary: Rectify reconciliation returns several raw errors without operation context while neighboring paths wrap context.
evidence: `StartramRetrieveReconciler.Reconcile` returns raw errors from `StartramSettingsSnapshot`, `GetStartramConfig`, and `UpdateConfig` (`return err`), but wraps other errors in the same function with descriptive context.
Other modules in the same batch consistently wrap boundary failures (`fmt.Errorf("...: %w", err)` in subsystem health and WiFi runtime), so diagnostics differ for similarly critical failures.
findings.suggestion: Normalize reconcile error exits to wrapped forms that include operation and target context (e.g., snapshot source, patp/config action), and apply the same wrapping rule across reconcile helpers.

findings.summary: Internal event channels use conflicting overflow/failure semantics, yielding unpredictable behavior under load.
evidence: `PublishSchedulePack` uses non-blocking send and returns `errSchedulePackBusFull` when saturated.
`updateBroadcast` handles full client channels with non-blocking send and returns aggregated drop errors via `errors.Join`.

`dockerListenerWithContext` sends to `eventBus` without a default overflow path; when buffer fills, producer blocks until consumer drains or context is canceled, producing stall behavior instead of explicit saturation error.
findings.suggestion: Define one shared backpressure strategy for internal buses (drop+error, bounded retry, or blocking-with-timeout) and apply it consistently, including explicit error/metric reporting for saturation in Docker event flow.

findings.summary: WiFi info refresh path silently no-ops in states that peer APIs treat as explicit errors.
evidence: `ConstructWifiInfo` returns no error and exits early on several failure-like states (nil service/state, unresolved device), so callers cannot distinguish success from skipped refresh.
Adjacent APIs (`IsWifiEnabled`, `ListWifiSSIDs`, `ConnectToWifi`, `DisconnectWifi`, `ToggleDevice`) explicitly return wrapped errors for comparable runtime precondition failures.
findings.suggestion: Return an error from `ConstructWifiInfo` (or a status enum) and propagate device-resolution/runtime precondition failures so WiFi API callers observe a consistent failure contract.
